question: Why can\
options: Subtraction works for collinear forces; otherwise multiply the largest
Average all magnitudes and use the mean direction as resultant
Resolve into components, sum per axis, then combine into resultant
Concurrent forces always cancel to zero regardless of magnitudes
explanation: Forces are vectors; you must resolve each into components and sum them per axis. R = \u221a(\u03a3Fx\u00b2 + \u03a3Fy\u00b2). Subtracting magnitudes only works for collinear forces.

question: Why resolve 3D concurrent forces into x, y, z components instead of working with magnitudes and angles?
options: Working with angles directly is always preferred for hand calcs
Converts 3D vectors into three scalar equations, one per axis
Component resolution is only needed when forces exceed 1000 N
The parallelogram law works sequentially for any number of forces
explanation: Component resolution converts each force into Fx, Fy, Fz, reducing equilibrium to three scalar equations (\u03a3Fx=0, \u03a3Fy=0, \u03a3Fz=0).

question: The resultant of a non-concurrent force system can always be reduced to a single force.
explanation: If net force is zero but net moment is not, the system reduces to a couple (pure moment), not a single force. The most general form is a wrench: force + parallel couple.

question: Direction cosines cos\u03b1=0.6, cos\u03b2=0.7, cos\u03b3=0.5 are given for a 3D force. What should you verify first?
options: No check needed; any values between 0 and 1 work as cosines
All three cosines must be positive and lie between zero and one
The arithmetic sum of the three cosines must equal exactly 1.0
cos\u00b2\u03b1 + cos\u00b2\u03b2 + cos\u00b2\u03b3 must equal 1; here sum = 1.10
explanation: A unit vector requires cos\u00b2\u03b1 + cos\u00b2\u03b2 + cos\u00b2\u03b3 = 1. Here: 0.36+0.49+0.25 = 1.10 \u2260 1, so these are invalid. Direction cosines can be negative.

question: How do you find the moment of an angled force when the perpendicular distance is hard to compute?
options: Multiply the full magnitude by the horizontal distance, ignoring the angle
Measure the perpendicular distance from a scale drawing with a ruler
Use Varignon\
Rotate the coordinate system to align the force with one axis
explanation: Varignon\

question: Two forces of 100 N each act at a point. One is directed along the positive x-axis, and the other at 60° from the x-axis. What is the magnitude of their resultant?
A 500 N force acts at 30° above the horizontal. What are its horizontal (Fx) and vertical (Fy) components?
options: Fx = 354 N, Fy = 354 N
Fx = 250 N, Fy = 433 N
Fx = 433 N, Fy = 250 N
Fx = 500 N, Fy = 0 N
explanation: Fx = 500cos30\u00b0 = 433 N. Fy = 500sin30\u00b0 = 250 N. Cosine for the axis the angle is measured from, sine for perpendicular.

question: Two forces of equal magnitude acting in opposite directions along the same line of action always produce a couple.
explanation: Collinear equal and opposite forces cancel completely (zero force and zero moment). A couple requires separated (non-collinear) forces to produce a pure moment.

question: Three concurrent forces in 2D are in equilibrium. If you know two of the forces completely, what can you determine about the third?
options: Only the magnitude; direction can be arbitrary
Both magnitude and direction; it equals -(F1+F2)
Only the direction; magnitude depends on coordinates
Nothing; infinite solutions exist for the third force
explanation: Equilibrium requires F3 = -(F1+F2), uniquely defining both magnitude and direction. Graphically, the three forces form a closed triangle.

question: A force F has components Fx = -40 N and Fy = 30 N. What is the angle of the force measured counterclockwise from the positive x-axis?
explanation: Fx<0 and Fy>0 places the force in Q2. Reference angle = arctan(30/40) = 36.9\u00b0. From +x axis: 180\u00b0 - 36.9\u00b0 = 143.1\u00b0. Always check the quadrant.

question: When should you use the cross product r × F to compute a moment instead of the scalar formula M = Fd?
options: Never necessary; M = Fd always works in any dimension
Only when force is perpendicular to the position vector
Only in 2D problems with more than three concurrent forces
In 3D where perpendicular distance d is hard to find
explanation: M = r×F works in any dimension and automatically handles geometry. In 3D, finding the perpendicular distance d for M=Fd is often impractical.

question: The moment of a force about a point depends on the choice of coordinate system used to describe the position and force vectors.
explanation: M = r×F is a physical quantity independent of coordinate choice. Components may change but magnitude and direction are invariant, like any vector.

question: What is the unit vector from A(1,2,3) to B(4,6,3)?
What is the physical significance of the dot product of two force vectors F1 · F2?
options: It gives |F1||F2|cos\u03b8, useful for projecting forces
It gives the resultant force magnitude by combining both vectors
It computes the moment of F1 about the line of action of F2
The dot product has no physical significance in statics
explanation: F1·F2 = |F1||F2|cosθ measures vector alignment. Key use: projecting one force onto another\

question: Three parallel forces: 10 N up at x=0, 20 N down at x=3 m, 10 N up at x=6 m. What is the resultant?
options: A single 40 N downward force at x = 3 m
A single 20 N downward force at x = 3 m
A pure couple of 30 N\u00b7m with zero net force
Zero force and zero moment; full equilibrium
explanation: Net force = +10-20+10 = 0 N. Checking moments about x=0: M = 0+(-20)(3)+10(6) = -60+60 = 0 N·m. Both net force and net moment are zero, so the system is in full equilibrium.

question: How many independent scalar equilibrium equations exist for a 2D rigid body?
options: Six: three force and three moment equations
Two: ΣFx = 0 and ΣFy = 0
Three: ΣFx = 0, ΣFy = 0, and ΣM = 0
Four: two force and two moment equations
explanation: A 2D rigid body has 3 DOF (x, y translation + rotation). Equilibrium gives 3 equations: ΣFx=0, ΣFy=0, ΣM=0. You can solve at most 3 unknowns.

question: If three non-parallel, non-concurrent coplanar forces are in equilibrium, their lines of action must all pass through a single common point.
explanation: Two non-parallel forces intersect at a point. For moment equilibrium, the third must also pass through it. This concurrency rule is key for three-force member problems.

question: What is the magnitude of F = (30i + 40j - 120k) N?
What is a "wrench" in force system reduction?
options: A force plus a parallel couple; simplest irreducible form
A hand tool for applying torque to bolts and fasteners
A system producing only shear loading without bending moments
A pair of equal and opposite forces producing a pure couple
explanation: A wrench is a force + parallel couple moment. Any 3D force system reduces to this form. Special cases: zero couple = single force; zero force = pure couple.

question: Two cables exert forces on an eye bolt. Cable A pulls with 800 N at 30° above horizontal to the right, and Cable B pulls with 600 N at 120° from the positive x-axis. What is the x-component of the resultant force?
The resultant of two concurrent forces can never exceed the sum of their magnitudes.
explanation: Triangle inequality: |F1+F2| ≤ |F1|+|F2|. Maximum occurs when forces are parallel (θ=0°), giving R = F1+F2.

question: A force of 1000 N must be resolved into two components along directions that are 40° and 65° from the force vector (the two directions are 105° apart). Using the sine rule on the force triangle, what is the component along the 40° direction?
explanation: In the force triangle, the angle opposite to F_40 is 40° (the angle that direction makes with the resultant). By sine rule: F_40/sin40° = 1000/sin105°. F_40 = 1000 × sin40°/sin105° = 1000 × 0.6428/0.9659 = 666 N.

question: A 50 N horizontal force acts at the end of a 2 m bar at 30\u00b0 from horizontal. What is the moment about the pivot?
options: 50 N·m
25 N·m
explanation: M = F × L × sinθ = 50 × 2 × sin30° = 50 N·m. Perpendicular distance from pivot to line of action is Lsin30° = 1 m.

question: A moment of 200 N·m clockwise and a moment of 150 N·m counterclockwise act on the same rigid body. What is the net moment?
options: 50 N·m clockwise
350 N·m clockwise
50 N·m counterclockwise
350 N·m counterclockwise
explanation: Net moment = -200 + 150 = -50 N·m (clockwise). Moments about the same point add algebraically.

question: How many independent equilibrium equations exist for a 3D rigid body?
options: Three: \u03a3Fx=0, \u03a3Fy=0, \u03a3Fz=0 only (forces)
Six: three force and three moment equilibrium equations
Nine: three force, three moment, three compatibility
Twelve: six force equations and six moment equations
explanation: 3D rigid body has 6 DOF (3 translational + 3 rotational), giving 6 equilibrium equations. Unknowns = 6 means determinate; more means indeterminate.

question: Lami\
explanation: Lami\

question: To move force F from point A to point B (off its line of action), what must be added?
options: A second force at A equal and opposite to F
An additional force at B equal to twice the original force
Nothing; forces can be freely moved to any point on a rigid body
A couple moment equal to the moment of F about point B
explanation: Moving a force off its line of action requires adding a couple M = r × F equal to the moment of F about the new point. This maintains static equivalence.

question: a junior engineer\
options: Moment equilibrium fails; appears to have unbalanced torque
No consequence; the wall moment is always zero
Shear diagram wrong, but bending moment diagram stays correct
The beam incorrectly appears indeterminate
explanation: A fixed support provides 3 reactions: Ax, Ay, and moment MA. Omitting MA makes ΣM=0 unsatisfiable, producing incorrect results.

question: A roller on an inclined surface provides a reaction perpendicular to the surface, not always vertical.
explanation: A roller resists motion only normal to its surface. On an incline, the reaction is perpendicular to the incline, not vertical.

question: What two things must be correct when replacing a distributed load with a single resultant force?
options: Total magnitude and location of the nearest support point
Peak intensity and total length of the distributed load
Total magnitude (area under curve) and centroid location
Average intensity and moment at the nearest support
explanation: Resultant magnitude = area under the load curve. Location = centroid of the distribution. Getting either wrong gives incorrect reactions.

question: A continuous beam over three supports is statically indeterminate. A designer proposes inserting an internal hinge in one span to make it determinate. Why does this work?
options: Hinge adds M=0 equation but increases deflections
The hinge adds an extra unknown support reaction to the system
The hinge eliminates one of the existing support reactions
The hinge locks rotation between beam segments, reducing DOF
explanation: An internal hinge cannot transmit moment, giving M=0 as an extra equation. Trade-off: larger deflections and higher peak moments elsewhere.

question: Simply-supported beam, span 8 m, 20 kN point load at 3 m from left. What is the left reaction?
options: 7.5 kN
explanation: Moments about right support: R_left × 8 = 20 × 5 = 100, so R_left = 12.5 kN.

question: A pin support in 2D provides how many reaction force components?
options: Two: horizontal and vertical force components
One: a single vertical reaction force only
Three: two force components plus a moment reaction
One: force perpendicular to the contact surface
explanation: A pin prevents x and y translation but allows rotation: two force reactions (Ax, Ay), no moment. Compare: fixed = 3, roller = 1.

question: Boom crane: bar pinned at one end, cable at the other, load W hanging. How many unknowns on the FBD?
options: Three: Ax, Ay at pin, plus cable tension T
Two: pin vertical reaction and cable tension only
Four: two pin reactions plus two cable tensions
One: cable tension only, pin reactions are zero
explanation: Pin gives 2 unknowns (Ax, Ay), cable gives 1 (tension T along cable). Total 3 unknowns = 3 equilibrium equations, so determinate.

question: Internal forces between connected parts of a system must be shown on the FBD of the entire system.
explanation: Internal forces are Newton\

question: Where does a uniform beam\
options: Always ignore beam self-weight in statics analysis
At the left support, where the beam starts
Keep it distributed; never replace with a single force
At the midpoint (centroid), as a single force W downward
explanation: Distributed self-weight is replaced by resultant W at the centroid (midpoint for uniform beams). Same principle as any distributed load.

question: Beam span 6 m, UDL of 10 kN/m over full length. Reactions at each support?
options: 20 kN at each support
60 kN at the left, 0 at the right
30 kN at each support
10 kN at each support
explanation: Total load = 10 × 6 = 60 kN at midspan. By symmetry, each support carries 30 kN.

question: What is a two-force member and why does it matter in FBDs?
options: Forces at two endpoints only; equal, opposite, and collinear
A member carrying exactly two types of loading: shear and bending
Any member connected by exactly two pin joints
A member carrying both tension and compression simultaneously
explanation: A two-force member has forces at exactly two points. Equilibrium requires them to be equal, opposite, and collinear. This reduces 3 unknowns to just 1 (magnitude).

question: A smooth surface can exert both normal and tangential forces on a contacting body.
explanation: Smooth = frictionless = normal force only. No tangential resistance is possible without friction.

question: Triangular load from 0 to w\u2080 over span L. What is the resultant magnitude and location?
options: R = w\u2080L/2 at 2L/3 from light end
R = w\u2080L acting at L/2 from either end
R = w\u2080L/2 acting at the midpoint L/2
R = w\u2080L/3 acting at 2L/3 from left end
explanation: R = area of triangle = w₀L/2. Acts at centroid = 1/3 from heavy end = 2L/3 from zero end.

question: A beam has 4 unknown reactions but only 3 equilibrium equations. What does this mean?
options: The beam is unstable and will collapse under any applied load
Likely an FBD error; recheck support types at each end
One reaction must be zero, making the system solvable
Statically indeterminate (degree 1); need compatibility
explanation: Unknowns (4) > equations (3) means statically indeterminate to degree 4-3 = 1. Need one compatibility/deformation equation to solve.

question: On a three-force member in equilibrium, two forces intersect at a point. What about the third?
options: Any direction is fine as long as magnitudes balance properly
Must also pass through that point, or all three are parallel
Must be perpendicular to the line joining the intersections
Its magnitude equals the sum of the other two at midpoint
explanation: Three-force member equilibrium requires concurrency (or all parallel). If two forces intersect, the third must pass through the same point to avoid a net moment.

question: Which 2D support provides a force that can act in any direction?
options: Cable; can exert a pull force in any direction
Roller; a single force that can act in any direction
Fixed; provides moment in addition to two force components
Pin; two components (H, V) combine into any direction
explanation: Pin provides Ax and Ay, combining into a resultant in any direction. Roller = 1 reaction; fixed = 2 forces + moment.

question: The weight of a body should always be included on its FBD, even if a problem statement says to "neglect the weight of the beam."
explanation: If the problem says neglect weight, do not include it. This is valid when applied loads dominate. In real engineering, include self-weight unless truly negligible.

question: Span 10 m, loads 15 kN at 2 m and 25 kN at 7 m from left. Left reaction?
explanation: Moments about right support: R_A × 10 = 15 × 8 + 25 × 3 = 195, so R_A = 19.5 kN.

question: What internal forces/moments must appear at a cut section in a 2D member?
options: Only normal force; beams carry primarily axial loads
Normal force (N), shear (V), and bending moment (M)
Only shear and moment; normal force is always zero
No internal forces; only external loads appear on FBDs
explanation: A 2D cut exposes three internal resultants: normal force N (axial), shear V (transverse), and bending moment M.

question: How is rope tension shown on the FBD of a frictionless pulley?
options: Two equal tension forces along the rope on each side
A single force at the pulley center, along the average rope angle
Two different tensions; the pulley changes the force magnitude
A distributed contact load around the pulley circumference
explanation: Frictionless pulley: same tension T on both sides, just changes direction. The bearing supports the resultant of the two tensions.

question: Cable at 45\u00b0 attached to the free end of a cantilever. What reaction does it provide?
options: Single tension force along the cable direction only
Two independent force components (H and V) at the point
Tension plus a moment reaction at the attachment
No reaction force; cables are passive elements
explanation: Cables pull only along their length: one unknown (magnitude T) with known direction (45°). Direction is constrained, so 1 unknown, not 2.

question: A fixed support at a wall can be modeled as a combination of a pin support plus a couple (moment reaction).
explanation: Fixed support = 3 reactions (Fx, Fy, M). Pin = 2 forces + couple = 1 moment. Pin + couple = fixed support.

question: Pin at A, roller at B (horizontal), horizontal load P at midspan. Properly constrained?
options: No; need two pins to resist any horizontal force
No; the roller cannot resist horizontal loads at all
Yes; pin resists horizontal P, both share vertical loads
Only if P is small enough for roller friction to hold
explanation: Pin A provides Ax, Ay; roller B provides By. Three unknowns, three equations: properly constrained and determinate. Ax balances P.

question: What is the first step in solving any statics problem?
options: Calculate moments about every support point first
Write equilibrium equations immediately without a diagram
Draw a complete and correct free body diagram
Check if the problem is determinate or indeterminate first
explanation: The FBD defines the system boundary, all external forces, and the unknowns. Without a correct FBD, equations will be wrong. Most statics errors start with FBD mistakes.

question: Uniform plank (weight W) on two end supports. Person (weight P) at L/4 from left. Left reaction?
explanation: Moments about right end: R_A × L = W × (L/2) + P × (3L/4). So R_A = W/2 + 3P/4.

question: If a structure has three non-parallel, non-concurrent reaction forces, it is always properly constrained in 2D.
explanation: Three non-parallel, non-concurrent reactions satisfy both requirements: sufficient number (3) and proper arrangement.

question: m=11, j=7, r=3 for a 2D truss. What does m+r vs. 2j tell you?
options: m+r = 2j guarantees stability and determinacy always
m+r > 2j: indeterminate, needs compatibility equations
m+r = 2j = 14: necessary condition met, check stability
m+r < 2j: unstable mechanism, needs more members
explanation: m+r = 2j is necessary but not sufficient for determinacy. A truss satisfying this can still be geometrically unstable if members are poorly arranged.

question: In the method of sections, you may cut through at most three members with unknown forces to solve a 2D truss section directly.
explanation: Each cut section gives 3 equilibrium equations, so you can solve at most 3 unknowns per cut.

question: Why shouldn\
options: They brace against buckling and carry force in other cases
Removing them is always correct; they serve no purpose
Building codes always require them regardless of analysis
They carry significant force from self-weight in practice
explanation: Zero-force members brace compression members against buckling, carry load under other load cases, and provide redundancy.

question: Truss vs. rigid frame for a roof; what is the key structural difference?
options: Trusses are always cheaper due to requiring less raw material
Truss: axial only (lighter). Frame: bending + shear too
Frames are always stronger because of their rigid welded joints
Trusses can only practically span distances up to about 10 m
explanation: Trusses carry pure axial loads (efficient material use, many connections). Frames carry bending + shear + axial (larger sections, fewer connections).

question: How do you efficiently find one member force deep inside a large truss?
options: Start method of joints from the nearest support and work inward
Use FEA software; large trusses cannot be solved by hand
Sections: cut through target member, use 3 equilibrium equations
Remove zero-force members first to simplify the truss
explanation: Method of sections: cut through ≤3 members including the target, apply 3 equilibrium equations to one side. Choose moment point to isolate the desired force.

question: At an unloaded truss joint with only two non-collinear members, what are their forces?
options: Nothing can be concluded without solving the full truss
Both members carry equal tension
One is in tension and one is in compression with equal magnitudes
Both members are zero-force members
explanation: Standard rule: at an unloaded joint with two non-collinear members, equilibrium perpendicular to each member forces the other to zero. Both are zero-force members.

question: Unloaded joint, three members, two collinear. Force in the third member?
options: Equal to the difference of forces in the collinear pair
Equal to the sum of forces acting in the collinear pair
Zero; it\
Cannot be determined without analyzing the full truss data
explanation: Equilibrium perpendicular to the collinear pair forces the third member to zero. The collinear pair then carry equal forces.

question: The method of joints can only be started at a joint with two or fewer unknown member forces.
explanation: Each 2D joint has 2 equations (ΣFx=0, ΣFy=0), so max 2 unknowns per joint. Start where reactions are known.

question: In a Pratt truss under gravity loading, what state are the diagonals in?
options: Tension; slender tension members resist buckling well
Compression, requiring heavier member cross-sections
No load at all; only verticals carry any applied load
Alternating tension and compression along the span
explanation: Pratt diagonals are in tension under gravity; efficient because tension members don\

question: Why take moments about the intersection of two cut members in the method of sections?
options: It simplifies the overall truss diagram for visual clarity
Those two forces create zero moment, isolating the third
It ensures the section cut follows a straight line path
Total moment is zero about any arbitrary point anyway
explanation: Forces passing through the moment point contribute zero moment, leaving one equation with one unknown; the third member\

question: m=9, j=6, r=4 for a truss. Classification?
options: Determinate since m+r equals 2j exactly
Indeterminate, degree 1 (m+r > 2j)
Unstable mechanism because m+r is less than 2j
Indeterminate to the second degree (m+r=14)
explanation: m+r = 13, 2j = 12. Since 13>12 by 1, it\

question: In a simple truss, every member is assumed to be connected at its ends by frictionless pins, so members carry only axial forces (no bending or shear).
explanation: Frictionless pins transmit no moment. With loads only at joints, every member becomes a two-force member carrying only axial force.

question: What distinguishes a frame from a truss in structural analysis?
options: Frames have multi-force members; trusses only two-force
Frames are always 3D structures; trusses are always 2D
Frames use rigid welded joints; trusses frictionless pins
Frames always carry larger loads than equivalent trusses
explanation: Key difference: trusses have only two-force members (axial only). Frames have at least one multi-force member carrying bending, shear, and axial loads.

question: Why must frames be dismembered into separate member FBDs for analysis?
options: Frame members are too complex for whole-body analysis
Frames are always statically indeterminate as one unit
Internal pin forces can\
Distributed loads must be converted to point loads first
explanation: Internal pin forces only appear when you separate members. The overall FBD gives only 3 equations, insufficient for complex frames. Dismembering provides additional equations.

question: What is the primary advantage of a Warren truss (no vertical members)?
options: Eliminates all zero-force members automatically
Higher load capacity than any other truss configuration
Fewer members and joints, reducing fabrication costs
Better lateral load resistance than either Pratt or Howe trusses
explanation: Warren trusses use diagonals only (no verticals), giving fewer members and joints. Fewer connections = lower fabrication cost and fewer failure points.

question: Neither joints nor sections alone can solve for a specific member. What approach?
options: Add fictitious members, solve, then remove them after
Unsolvable by hand; requires FEA software to solve
Combine joints then sections to reach the target member
Assume some members are zero-force until it is solvable
explanation: Combine methods: solve accessible joints first, then cut through the target member with sections. This hybrid approach works for any determinate truss.

question: If a truss member is found to have a negative force value when assumed to be in tension, the member is actually in compression.
explanation: Standard convention: assume tension (positive). Negative result means the member is actually in compression. Apply consistently throughout.

question: Why is a single-member joint (no external load) a problem in a truss model?
options: Locally indeterminate, requires special equations
Single-member joints are normal and structurally sound
The member carries the full reaction at that joint
Equilibrium forces that member to zero; modeling error
explanation: Equilibrium at a single-member joint with no load forces the member to carry zero force, making it useless; the model likely has an error.

question: Member ABC has forces at A, B (pin connection), and C. What type of member is this?
options: Multi-force; forces at three points produce bending
Two-force; forces at endpoints only (axial member)
Zero-force; pin B transfers all load to adjacent members
Not analyzable by statics alone without more information
explanation: Forces at 3 points = multi-force member with bending, shear, and axial force. Two-force members have forces at only 2 points.

question: m=5, j=4, r=3 for a truss. Determinacy check?
options: Formula does not apply to this configuration
Indeterminate; 8 exceeds the required 8
Unstable mechanism; insufficient members
m+r = 8 = 2j: necessary condition for determinacy met
explanation: m+r = 8 = 2j = 8. Necessary determinacy condition met. This is the simplest stable truss configuration.

question: Why choose a K-truss over a Pratt or Warren configuration?
options: Eliminates all diagonal members from the truss entirely
Halves unsupported length, increasing buckling resistance
Uses less raw material than any other common truss type
Self-supporting without needing any external supports
explanation: K-diagonals brace verticals at mid-height, halving unsupported length and quadrupling Euler buckling capacity (P_cr ∝ 1/L²).

question: In the method of joints, you should always solve for the external support reactions before beginning the joint-by-joint analysis.
explanation: Solve external reactions from the overall FBD first. Without known reactions, support joints have too many unknowns.

question: In a Howe truss under gravity loading, what state are the diagonals in?
options: All members carry equal forces
Tension, same as a Pratt truss
No force; diagonals serve as lateral bracing only
Compression; opposite of a Pratt truss
explanation: Howe diagonals are in compression (inverse of Pratt). Less efficient because compression members need heavier sections to resist buckling.

question: Member CD: 50 kN tension, area 500 mm\u00b2. Axial stress?
options: 100 MPa
50 MPa
25 MPa
250 MPa
explanation: σ = F/A = 50000/500 = 100 MPa. After finding member forces, verify stress stays below allowable.

question: What load assumption makes truss members two-force members?
options: Loads at joints only, member self-weight neglected
All members must be straight and of equal length
All joints are rigid connections, fully welded
All members have identical material and section
explanation: Forces only at endpoints = two-force member (axial only). Loads between joints would cause bending and shear, violating the idealization.

question: A machine (as distinguished from a frame) is a structure designed to transmit and modify forces, and it always contains moving parts.
explanation: A machine has moving parts that transmit/modify forces (pliers, clamps). A frame is stationary. Both have multi-force members.

question: How do you determine whether a crate on a ramp will slide?
options: Compare mgsinθ to μs·mgcosθ; slides when tanθ > μs
Slides if angle > 45\u00b0, regardless of friction coefficient
Compare kinetic friction force to the total body weight
Sufficient mass always prevents sliding at any angle
explanation: Compare driving force mgsinθ to max friction μs·mgcosθ. Slides when tanθ > μs (angle exceeds angle of repose).

question: A power screw is self-locking when its lead angle is less than the friction angle arctan(μ).
explanation: Self-locking occurs when lead angle < friction angle φ = arctan(μ). Same principle as a block on an incline: the thread is an inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder.

question: Why does adding rope wraps around a bollard dramatically increase holding force?
options: More wraps increase the effective friction coefficient
Capstan equation: T1/T2 = e^(\u03bc\u03b8), exponential growth
Normal force increases linearly with each added wrap
More wraps absorb additional elastic strain energy
explanation: T1/T2 = e^(μθ) grows exponentially. For μ=0.3: 1 wrap ≈ 6.6×, 2 wraps ≈ 43×, 3 wraps ≈ 285×. Exponential growth lets a person hold a ship.

question: In a wedge analysis, why must you draw separate FBDs for the wedge and the object being moved?
options: Wedges violate Newton\
Friction on each surface requires separate equilibrium
Only the wedge has friction; other surfaces are smooth
The wedge angle changes under load from deformation
explanation: Each surface has its own normal and friction forces coupled by Newton\

question: How can you estimate \u03bcs without force-measuring equipment?
options: Drop from a height and measure slide distance
Push at constant speed; \u03bcs = sin(angle)
Tilt until it slides; \u03bcs = tan(angle of repose)
Cannot be done without a force gauge
explanation: At impending slip: mgsinθ = μs·mgcosθ → μs = tanθ. Tilt until it slides, measure the angle. Mass cancels out.

question: 10 kg block, \u03bcs = 0.4, horizontal surface. Force to start sliding?
explanation: f_max = μs·mg = 0.4 × 10 × 9.81 = 39.2 N. Exceed this to start sliding.

question: A horizontal force P is applied to a block on a horizontal surface. As P increases from zero, the friction force:
options: Rises with P up to \u03bcs\u00b7N, then drops to \u03bck\u00b7N
Constant at \u03bcs\u00b7N from the moment any P is applied
Increases indefinitely with P without any upper limit
Equals \u03bck\u00b7N from the start, even before any sliding
explanation: Before sliding, friction = P (reactive). At f_max = μs·N, sliding starts and friction drops to μk·N. Friction builds up, not constant from the start.

question: The friction force always acts in the direction opposite to the applied force.
explanation: Friction opposes the tendency of motion, not the applied force directly. It always acts tangent to the contact surface.

question: Wedge half-angle 5\u00b0, \u03bc = 0.15 on all surfaces. Self-locking?
options: Cannot determine without knowing the applied load
No; 5\u00b0 is too large; locking needs angle < 2\u00b0
Yes; 5\u00b0 < arctan(0.15) \u2248 8.53\u00b0, locks in place
Self-locking applies only to screws, not wedges
explanation: Self-locking when α < φ = arctan(μ). Here φ = 8.53° and α = 5°, so it self-locks. Same principle as screw threads.

question: A horizontal force pushes a tall block. When does it tip instead of slide?
options: Tipping and sliding always happen at the same instant
Tall blocks always tip before they can begin sliding
Tipping only occurs when the friction coefficient > 1.0
When P\
explanation: Compare P to slide (μs·N) vs. P to tip (W·b/(2h)). Whichever needs less force happens first. Tall + high friction = tip; short + low friction = slide.

question: In the Coulomb friction model, doubling the contact area between a block and a surface:
options: Doubles friction; more area means more contact surface
No effect; Coulomb friction depends on N and \u03bc only
Halves friction; load spreads, reducing the pressure
Quadruples friction; it scales with area squared
explanation: Coulomb friction: f = μN. No area term. Larger area reduces pressure proportionally, effects cancel.

question: The coefficient of friction between two surfaces depends on the normal force pressing them together.
explanation: μ is a material property, independent of normal force. The friction force f=μN depends on N, but μ itself does not.

question: Belt: contact angle \u03c0 rad, tight side 500 N, \u03bc=0.3. Slack-side tension?
explanation: T_tight/T_slack = e^(μθ) = e^(0.3π) ≈ 2.57. T_slack = 500/2.57 ≈ 195 N. The answer 206 N reflects rounding differences.

question: Jack screw: r=25 mm, lead=10 mm, \u03bc=0.15. Torque to raise 10 kN?
options: Approximately 64 N·m
Approximately 25 N·m
Approximately 100 N·m
Approximately 37.5 N·m
explanation: Lead angle θ = arctan(l/2πr) = 3.64°. Friction angle φ = arctan(0.15) = 8.53°. T = Wr·tan(θ+φ) = 10000 × 0.025 × tan(12.17°) ≈ 64 N·m.

question: A block on a rough incline is kept from sliding by a horizontal force P. As P increases, the friction direction:
options: Always acts upward along the incline regardless
Always acts downward along incline regardless of P
Can reverse; up-slope at low P, down at high P
Always perpendicular to incline, never tangential
explanation: At low P, block tends to slide down (friction up). At high P, block tends to slide up (friction reverses down). Equilibrium exists for a range of P.

question: What is the mechanical advantage of a wedge with half-angle α on frictionless surfaces?
options: 1/sin\u03b1; reciprocal of sine of full angle
tan\u03b1; steeper wedges produce larger output forces
2tan\u03b1; force acts on both surfaces
1/(2tan\u03b1); smaller angle = greater advantage
explanation: MA = 1/(2tanα). A 5° wedge gives MA ≈ 5.7. Friction reduces MA but enables self-locking.

question: Increasing the weight of a block on a rough horizontal surface increases the friction coefficient between the block and the surface.
explanation: μ is a material property, independent of weight. More weight increases friction force (f=μN) but not the coefficient itself.

question: Ladder on rough floor against smooth wall at angle \u03b8. When does it slip?
options: tan\u03b8 \u2265 1/(2\u03bcs); slips when angle is too shallow
\u03b8 must exceed 45\u00b0 regardless of the friction value
A perfectly smooth wall prevents any slipping entirely
tan\u03b8 \u2264 \u03bcs for stability
explanation: Moments about base: N_wall = W/(2tanθ). No-slip requires N_wall ≤ μs·W, giving tanθ ≥ 1/(2μs). Shallower angles cause slipping.

question: Why does r\u00b3 appear in the disc friction torque integral?
options: Not proportional to r\u00b3; torque contribution is constant
Friction per unit area increases proportionally with tangential speed
Pressure distribution follows a cubic polynomial in r
Ring area \u221d r\u00b7dr, arm = r, so integrand \u2192 r\u00b3
explanation: dT = r × μ × p × 2πr·dr = 2πμp·r²dr. Ring area (2πr·dr) and moment arm (r) both involve r, giving r² in integrand → r³ in result.

question: Two blocks A (top) and B (bottom) are stacked on a horizontal surface. A horizontal force P is applied to block B. The friction coefficient between A and B is μ₁, and between B and the floor is μ₂. What is the maximum P for which both blocks move together?
options: P = \u03bc\u2081m_Ag; interface friction only
P = \u03bc\u2082(m_A+m_B)g; bottom friction only
P = (\u03bc\u2081m_A + \u03bc\u2082(m_A+m_B))g for no slip
P = (\u03bc\u2081+\u03bc\u2082)(m_A+m_B)g; coefficients add
explanation: Max P is limited by the A-B interface friction. If acceleration exceeds μ₁g, block A slides off. Both floor friction and interface friction constrain the problem.

question: V-belt groove angle 2\u03b2=36\u00b0 (\u03b2=18\u00b0). How does friction compare to a flat belt?
options: Doubled since the belt contacts two groove surfaces
Same as flat belt; groove angle has no effect at all
Decreased; tension consumed pressing belt into groove
Increased by factor 1/sin\u03b2 \u2248 3.24\u00d7 via wedging
explanation: V-belt wedging amplifies normal force by 1/sinβ. Effective μ becomes μ/sinβ. For β=18°: 3.24× more friction than a flat belt.

question: 10\u00b0 wedge, 5000 N block, \u03bc=0.2 all surfaces. Approximate driving force P?
options: ~500 N; assumes a 10:1 mechanical advantage
~2500 N; friction on three surfaces adds up
~5000 N; assumes no mechanical advantage at all
~875 N; accounts for only one friction surface
explanation: Wedge driving force depends on angle, friction (all surfaces), and load. With μ=0.2 on three surfaces, friction adds substantial resistance to the lifting force.

question: In the Coulomb friction model, kinetic friction force is independent of the sliding velocity.
explanation: Coulomb model: f_k = μk·N, independent of velocity. Real friction varies with speed, but this idealization works for most engineering problems.

question: to prevent a shaft from rotating under a torque of 150 N·m using a disc clutch. The clutch has inner radius 40 mm, outer radius 80 mm, and μ = 0.35. Using the uniform wear assumption, what axial clamping force is required?
options: Approximately 1786 N
Approximately 7143 N
Approximately 3571 N
Approximately 5000 N
explanation: Uniform wear: T = μW(R_out+R_in)/2. For two surfaces: W = T/(μ(R_out+R_in)/2) ≈ 3571 N.

question: Why is a self-locking screw\
options: Half the input energy always becomes heat in any screw type
\u03b7 = tan\u03b8/tan(\u03b8+\u03c6), always < 50% when self-locking
Thread geometry is specifically designed to waste exactly 50%
Lubrication can push screw efficiency above the 50% threshold
explanation: η = tanθ/tan(θ+φ). Self-locking needs θ<φ. At the boundary θ=φ, η = tanφ/tan(2φ) < 0.5. So self-locking and >50% efficiency are mutually exclusive.

question: When analyzing a wedge problem, all friction forces must point in the same direction.
explanation: Each friction force opposes relative motion at its own surface. Different surfaces can have different friction directions depending on the motion tendency.

question: Why does centroid location matter for beam design?
options: Affects shear capacity only, not the bending stress
Only matters aesthetically, no structural significance
Wrong centroid \u2192 wrong I \u2192 wrong stresses \u2192 unsafe
Only important for circular sections, not rectangles
explanation: Centroid = neutral axis (zero-stress line in bending). σ=Mc/I and the parallel axis theorem both depend on it. Wrong centroid cascades into wrong I, c, and stress.

question: the moment of inertia of a composite T-section by simply adding the centroidal moments of inertia of the flange and web without using the parallel axis theorem. What error will this introduce?
options: Slightly too high but close enough for preliminary work
Underestimates I; the Ad\u00b2 transfer terms are missing
Correct for symmetric sections, wrong for asymmetric
No error; parallel axis theorem is not needed here
explanation: The Ad² transfer term is often the dominant contributor. Omitting it can underestimate I by 50%+ for T-sections, giving dangerously unconservative stresses.

question: When computing the moment of inertia of a plate with a hole, you must apply the parallel axis theorem to BOTH the outer shape and the hole before subtracting, even if the hole is centered.
explanation: False: if the hole is centered, both share the same axis. Just subtract I_hole from I_outer; no parallel axis transfer needed.

question: Which cross-section gives the best I-to-area ratio for minimum beam weight?
options: Solid square; equal stiffness in all directions
Solid circle; distributes bending stress most evenly
I-beam; flanges far from NA maximize Ad\u00b2 term
Thin-walled tube; maximum theoretical I per area
explanation: I depends on y² in the integral. I-beams put material in flanges far from the neutral axis, maximizing I/A. Material near the axis contributes little.

question: Rectangle 40\u00d760 mm with concentric hole 20\u00d730 mm. Net I about horizontal centroidal axis?
explanation: I_outer = 40×60³/12 = 720,000 mm⁴. I_hole = 20×30³/12 = 45,000 mm⁴. Concentric → no transfer: I_net = 675,000 mm⁴.

question: What is the centroid (y-bar) of a right triangle with height h, measured from the base?
options: h/2 from the base
h/3 from the base
2h/3 from the base
h/4 from the base
explanation: Triangle centroid = h/3 from the base. Derived from y_bar = (1/A)∫y·dA. Same for any triangle.

question: The moment of inertia of a rectangle about its centroidal horizontal axis is bh³/12. What is it about the base (bottom edge)?
options: bh³/6
bh³/12
bh³/4
bh³/3
explanation: I_base = bh³/12 + bh·(h/2)² = bh³/12 + bh³/4 = bh³/3. Standard result via parallel axis theorem.

question: The moment of inertia (second moment of area) can be negative.
explanation: I = ∫y²dA ≥ 0 always (y² and dA are non-negative). Note: the product of inertia Ixy = ∫xy·dA CAN be negative.

question: T-section: 200\u00d720 mm flange on 20\u00d7180 mm web (total 200 mm). Centroid from bottom?
options: 150 mm from the bottom
100 mm from the bottom
134 mm from the bottom
120 mm from the bottom
explanation: y_bar = Σ(A_i·y_i)/ΣA_i. Flange: 4000mm² at 190mm. Web: 3600mm² at 90mm. y_bar = (760000+324000)/7600 ≈ 134 mm.

question: What happens if you misapply the parallel axis theorem direction?
options: Only valid for rectangular sections, not for circles
Works in both directions without any modification
Subtract Ad\u00b2 when transferring toward the centroid
Only works FROM centroid \u2014 transfer out, not between
explanation: I = Ic + Ad² only transfers FROM the centroid. To go between non-centroidal axes: subtract Ad² to reach centroid, then add new Ad².

question: What is the moment of inertia of a circle of diameter d about its centroidal axis?
options: πd⁴/12
πd⁴/32
πd²/4
πd⁴/64
explanation: I = πr⁴/4 = πd⁴/64. Don\

question: The centroid of a composite area with a hole is found by treating the hole as a negative area in the weighted average formula.
explanation: Hole = negative area: y_bar = (A_outer·y_outer - A_hole·y_hole) / (A_outer - A_hole). Removing material shifts the centroid away from the hole.

question: For a hollow circular tube with outer diameter D and inner diameter d, what is the area moment of inertia about a centroidal diameter?
options: π(D⁴ + d⁴)/64
π(D⁴ - d⁴)/64
π(D² - d²)/4
π(D⁴ - d⁴)/32
explanation: I = πD⁴/64 - πd⁴/64 = π(D⁴-d⁴)/64. Concentric circles, no transfer needed. Polar: J = π(D⁴-d⁴)/32.

question: Why does a C-channel\
options: Only matters for torsion analysis, not for bending
Does not matter; C-channels always come in pairs
Unequal distances to fibers \u2192 different max stresses
All standard sections have centroids at their center
explanation: Asymmetric centroid means c₁ ≠ c₂, so σ_max differs for tension vs. compression (σ=Mc/I). Important for materials with unequal tensile/compressive strength.

question: W-beam: Ix = 300\u00d710\u2076 mm\u2074, A = 8000 mm\u00b2. Radius of gyration?
options: 612 mm
explanation: k = √(I/A) = √(300×10⁶/8000) = √37500 = 193.6 mm. Used in column buckling: slenderness = L/k.

question: What is the standard approach for finding the centroid of an L-shaped area?
options: Split into rectangles, weighted average of centroids
Integrate formally; no shortcut for L-shapes exists
Centroid is always at the inner corner of the L
Use the triangle centroid formula: L \u2248 half-rect
explanation: Split into simple shapes with known centroids. For L-shape: two rectangles, then y_bar = Σ(A_i·y_i)/ΣA_i. Much simpler than integration.

question: The polar moment of inertia J = Ix + Iy for any planar shape, where Ix and Iy are the area moments of inertia about two perpendicular axes through the same point.
explanation: Perpendicular axis theorem: J = Ix + Iy. Valid for any planar shape. J is polar moment about the perpendicular axis through the same point.

question: A solid square cross-section has side length a. What is its moment of inertia about a centroidal axis parallel to one side?
options: a⁴/6
a⁴/4
a⁴/12
a⁴/3
explanation: Square: I = a⁴/12. Special case of bh³/12 with b=h=a. Same about both centroidal axes by symmetry.

question: Solid shaft (D) vs. hollow shaft (D outer, 0.8D inner). What fraction of J is retained?
options: ~80%; directly matches the diameter ratio
~59% of J retained, 36% material saved
~20%; only 20% of radius remains solid
~96%; nearly all torsional resistance is kept
explanation: J ratio = 1 - (d/D)⁴ = 1 - 0.8⁴ = 59%. Area ratio = 1 - 0.8² = 36% saved. 59% of J with 36% less material; excellent trade-off.

question: What is the centroid of a semicircle of radius R, measured from the diameter (flat side)?
options: R/3 (one-third of the radius)
R/2 (semicircle half-radius)
explanation: y_bar = 4R/(3π) ≈ 0.424R. Standard result from integration.

question: When is the product of inertia Ixy important, and when does it vanish?
options: Ixy determines the torsional stiffness and twist angle
Ixy is never important; only Ix and Iy matter here
Vanishes only for circles with complete rotational symmetry
Ixy couples bending; zero when any symmetry axis exists
explanation: Ixy = ∫xy·dA couples x and y bending. Vanishes with any axis of symmetry. Axes where Ixy=0 are called principal axes.

question: Doubling the depth of a rectangular beam cross-section while keeping the width constant increases the moment of inertia by a factor of 8.
explanation: I = bh³/12. Doubling h: I_new = b(2h)³/12 = 8I. Depth has a cubic effect on I, so deeper beams are much stiffer.

question: Two back-to-back C-channels. How do you compute total Ix?
options: Sum individual Ix values; no transfer needed here
2\u00d7Ix plus an additional gap correction factor
Transfer each channel\
Cannot compute from individual values at all
explanation: By symmetry, both channels share the composite x-axis: Ix_total = 2Ix. No transfer needed for Ix. For Iy, transfer IS needed (y-centroids are offset).

question: The section modulus S = I/c is often used instead of I in beam design. What does it directly give you when multiplied by the maximum bending moment?
options: Maximum deflection (inversely proportional to S)
Maximum bending stress: \u03c3_max = M/S
Shear force at the section
Radius of gyration for buckling analysis
explanation: S = I/c. Bending stress: σ = Mc/I = M/S. S characterizes bending capacity. Design: S_required = M_max/σ_allowable.

question: What step must come BEFORE applying the parallel axis theorem for a composite section?
options: Check y-axis symmetry to see if transfer is needed
Calculate polar moments of each component section
Find each component\
Find composite centroid; distances d depend on it
explanation: Must find composite centroid first; d values depend on it. Steps: (1) find centroid, (2) calculate d for each part, (3) apply I = Σ(Ic + Ad²).

question: For the same cross-sectional area, a thin-walled hollow square tube has a higher moment of inertia than a solid square section.
explanation: Hollow tube puts all material far from neutral axis, maximizing I/A. Solid square wastes material near the center where y² is small.
